notes.note: Xeno (Xenon) of Athens, an agent who handled funds for Cicero, especially the allowance of his son studying there; the "nomen" is the account or debt to be settled. The Epirote affair concerns property and money in Epirus, where Atticus held large estates.
The nephew of L. Cornelius Balbus the elder (Caesar's chief financial agent), distinguished here by "the younger" from his more prominent uncle, who reappears in section 4.
Aulus Hirtius, a literary Caesarian (later consul of 43 BC) who continued Caesar's commentaries; here he is reported feuding at dinner parties over Cicero's loyalty to Caesar.
"The king" (rex) is Cicero's bitter shorthand for Caesar the dictator; the word carried for Romans the deepest opprobrium, and Cicero uses it to mark Caesar's autocracy.
Porcia, daughter of Cato Uticensis and wife of M. Brutus, had recently died; Cicero composed a laudatio (funeral eulogy) for her, copies of which he is circulating to her kinsmen.

Cn. Domitius Ahenobarbus the younger, Porcia's nephew (his mother was Cato's sister) and a future associate of Brutus and Cassius; a natural recipient of the eulogy of his kinswoman. Identification is by context, the standard reading of this passage.

A Roman public auction (auctio) was announced in advance by a posted bill (proscriptio) listing the goods and date; Cicero is arranging the sale of property, with Balbus and the jurist Ofilius preparing the inventory.
Aulus Ofilius (Cicero's spelling "Offilius"), an eminent jurist, pupil of Servius Sulpicius and friend of Caesar, here helping draw up the auction inventory.
